title: Non-existent operators like =+ should be avoided
description: Non-existent operators like "=+" should not be usedAnalyze your codeintentionality-clearreliabilityBugWhy is this an issue?Using operator pairs (=+,=-, or=!) that look like reversed single operators (+=,-=or!=) is confusing. They compile and run but do not produce the same result as their mirrored counterpart.int target = -5; int num = 3; target =- num; // Noncompliant: target = -3. Is that the intended behavior? target =+ num; // Noncompliant: target = 3This rule raises an issue when=+,=-, or=!are used without any space between the operators and when there is at least one whitespace after.Replace the operators with a single one if that is the intentionint target = -5; int num = 3; target -= num; // target = -8Or fix the spacing to avoid confusionint target = -5; int num = 3; target = -num
recommendation: Refactor to eliminate the flagged pattern.

logic.triggering_conditions: Assignment nodes containing confusing operator patterns like =+, =-, =! followed by whitespace
logic.reasoning: Detects confusing operator patterns that look like reversed compound operators. The pattern '\w+\s*=[+\-!]\s+' matches variable assignments followed by =+, =-, or =! with whitespace after, which are confusing and likely errors. Targets Assignment nodes to focus on actual assignment statements.

should_trigger: target =+ num;  // looks like += but assigns +num
target =- num;  // looks like -= but assigns -num
flag =! condition;  // looks like != but assigns !condition
